title: Trump's Term Echoes a Grim Past: The Legacy of Woodrow Wilson
content:
In 1912, Woodrow Wilson was able to garner a groundswell of support from Black voters by making a bold promise of absolute fair dealing and assistance. However, by the end of his presidential term, it became painfully clear that Wilson’s promises of racial advancement would ring completely hollow. Instead of helping, he packed his cabinet with white supremacists and actively allowed the segregation of the federal workforce. He even hosted a controversial White House screening of a film that openly glorified the Ku Klux Klan, deeply betraying the prominent Black leaders who had initially supported him. Today, more than a century later, Wilson’s legacy of broken promises has taken on new resonance among historians who are analyzing modern political administrations.

Many experts see distinct and troubling parallels between Wilson’s abandonment of Black Americans and the policies of President Donald Trump's recent administration. Critics point out that Trump’s assailing of diversity policies and occasional use of racist imagery strongly mirror Wilson’s historical efforts to cement a strict racial hierarchy. Despite these criticisms, Trump and his supporters vehemently argue that his administration has done more for Black Americans than any president since Abraham Lincoln. They frequently highlight his support for criminal justice reform and increased funding for Historically Black Colleges and Universities as proof of his positive impact. Nevertheless, researchers warn that casually elevating subtle racist rhetoric, much like Wilson did, can have devastating generational effects on minority communities.
